A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today’s dollars. For NVIDIA, this uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach built on cash flow projections. NVIDIA’s latest twelve month free cash flow is about $97.22b. Analysts provide free cash flow estimates out to 2031, with Simply Wall St extrapolating beyond the first few years. By 2035, projected free cash flow reaches $505.18b, with interim years such as 2030 and 2031 at $376.91b and $410.18b respectively, all in dollar terms. When those projected cash flows are discounted back using this model, the estimated intrinsic value for NVIDIA comes out at about $188.88 per share. Compared with the current share price of $182.08, the DCF output suggests the stock is around 3.6% undervalued, which is a relatively small gap. NVIDIA is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act. For a profitable company like NVIDIA, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to see how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. A higher P/E usually reflects stronger growth expectations or lower perceived risk, while a lower P/E can point to more modest growth assumptions or higher uncertainty. NVIDIA currently trades on a P/E of 36.8x. That sits below the Semiconductor industry average of 39.1x and well below the peer group average of 92.9x, which indicates that some comparable names trade at much richer earnings multiples. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for NVIDIA is 45.8x. This is a proprietary estimate of what a more tailored P/E might look like, based on factors such as earnings growth, industry, profit margins, market cap and company specific risks. Compared with simple peer or industry averages, the Fair Ratio aims to provide a more company specific view instead of assuming all chip stocks deserve the same multiple. When you compare the Fair Ratio of 45.8x with the current P/E of 36.8x, NVIDIA appears undervalued on this metric. P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? You access Narratives on the Community page, where millions of investors share their own numbers and reasoning, and you can quickly compare different fair values with the current US$182.08 share price to judge whether the stock fits your view. Narratives update automatically when new information arrives, so if NVIDIA reports results or there is fresh news on export controls or product launches, the forecasts and fair values behind each Narrative move with it. For example, one NVIDIA Narrative currently anchors on a fair value of about US$171 per share while another sits closer to US$305 per share. This shows how different assumptions about long term AI growth, margins and P/E can lead to very different conclusions, and gives you a clear range to think about before making your own call. For NVIDIA, however, we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading NVIDIA Narratives: These give you ready made, quantified stories that sit behind very different views on fair value, while still using the same current share price of about US$182.08 as the reference point. This lets you quickly see how bullish and more cautious assumptions translate into numbers and decide which feels closer to your own expectations for AI demand, margins and capital returns. Implied valuation gap to current price: about 32.1% below this fair value Analysts model very strong revenue growth and high profit margins over several years, with earnings reaching US$300.5b by about April 2029 and a future P/E of 28.9x. The story leans on a long running AI infrastructure buildout, regular new chip platforms and a growing full stack offer across hardware, networking and software that aims to keep customers closely tied to NVIDIA. Key risks focus on export controls, in house chips at large customers, manufacturing and power constraints, plus the possibility that heavy spending and a fast product cadence squeeze margins if demand does not stay as strong as expected. Implied valuation gap to current price: about 28.4% above this fair value This view still assumes solid growth from data centers, gaming, professional visualization and automotive, but couples that with a long run net margin of 40% and a high future P/E of 60x. The narrative highlights NVIDIA's strong position in AI hardware, gaming GPUs and platforms like DRIVE and Omniverse, while questioning how much of that potential is already reflected in the share price. Main risks include tougher competition in GPUs, pricing that could put off consumers, regulatory hurdles on deals and ongoing supply chain issues that might restrict shipments or hand ground to rivals. Together these two Narratives frame a wide but clearly quantified range around NVIDIA, and can be a useful starting point before you adjust the assumptions to match your own view of the company and the AI opportunity. title: Nvidia vs. Micron: Which AI Chip Stock Has More Upside Potential? - The Motley Fool
content: Some of the fastest-growing companies in the world operate in the semiconductor industry, and it's all thanks to artificial intelligence (AI). Developing artificial intelligence (AI) requires substantial computing power, which is why it typically occurs in large, centralized data centers. This infrastructure includes thousands of specialized chips called graphics processing units (GPUs) specifically designed for data-intensive AI workloads. High-bandwidth memory (HBM) is also a critical component in the hardware stack because it stores data in a ready state until GPUs are ready to process it. A low memory capacity can cause bottlenecks and force GPUs to pause their workloads while they wait to receive more data, whereas a high capacity keeps data flowing smoothly to unlock maximum processing speeds. Nvidia (NVDA +2.59%) makes the world's best GPUs, while Micron Technology (MU 0.23%) makes some of the highest-capacity HBM. Both companies have soared in value thanks to the AI boom, but which one has more upside potential from here? Nvidia has led the market for AI data center chips since it launched the H100 GPU in 2022, which was based on its Hopper architecture. Today, the company's Blackwell-based GB300 GPU delivers up to 50 times more performance than the H100 in certain configurations, so the pace of innovation has been staggering. Our experts' top-rated cash back card just got even better. For a limited time, earn a $250 bonus after spending just $500 in the first 3 months. In the second half of this year, Nvidia will start shipping commercial quantities of its new Vera Rubin semiconductor platform, which includes the Rubin GPU, the Vera CPU, and a slate of new networking components. The company says it will be so powerful that developers can train AI models using 75% fewer GPUs, resulting in a whopping 90% reduction in inference token costs. Inference tokens can be words, symbols, or images generated whenever a user prompts an AI chatbot or agent. Reducing token costs could drive a significant increase in AI usage while also boosting AI providers' profit margins. Both things are likely to bolster demand for Nvidia's GPUs. One little-known company, called an "Indispensable Monopoly" owns the technology Nvidia, AMD, and Intel cannot function without. And it is still just a fraction of Nvidia’s size. We just released a brand-new report with the full story and the company’s name. Nvidia generated a record $215.9 billion in revenue during its fiscal year 2026 (ended Jan. 25), along with earnings of $4.77 per share. Its stock currently trades at a price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 36.1, which is a substantial discount to its 10-year average of 61.6. Wall Street estimates that Nvidia will grow its earnings to $8.29 per share during fiscal 2027 (according to Yahoo! Finance), placing its stock at an even more attractive forward P/E ratio of 21.3. Nvidia stock would have to soar by 189% over the next year just for its P/E ratio to align with its 10-year average of 61.6. That isn't guaranteed to happen, but the stock could still deliver significant upside even if it were to fall short. Micron's flagship HBM3E memory solution for the data center provides up to 50% more capacity than competing hardware while consuming 30% less energy. The net result is faster processing speeds with lower costs, which is why Nvidia is using it alongside its Blackwell GB300 GPUs. But Micron recently launched its HBM4 solution, which offers 60% more capacity than its HBM3E solution, in addition to a 20% improvement in energy efficiency. It was actually designed for the Vera Rubin platform, so the partnership between Nvidia and Micron is about to enter its next phase. But Micron also has a massive AI opportunity outside the data center. It's one of the world's top suppliers of memory for personal computers (PCs) and smartphones, and these devices are gradually processing more AI workloads. The company says PCs with agentic AI capabilities require up to 32 gigabytes of dynamic random access memory (DRAM), which is twice as much as non-AI PCs. More memory means more money for Micron. Micron recently reported its operating results for its fiscal 2026 second quarter (ended Feb. 26). The company generated a record $23.9 billion in total revenue, up an eye-popping 196% year over year. Micron's earnings also exploded higher by 756% during the second quarter, and management is forecasting a staggering 1,025% earnings growth in the current third quarter. Micron stock is now trading at a P/E ratio of just 17.7 and a forward P/E ratio of 6.5 based on Wall Street's consensus estimate for the company's full-year fiscal 2026 earnings. Therefore, despite the company's blistering growth, its stock is significantly cheaper than the S&P 500 (^GSPC 0.11%), which trades at a P/E of 23.5 as of April 6. Micron stock is clearly much cheaper than Nvidia stock, so in theory, it could deliver more upside from here. However, memory prices are heavily inflated across all segments right now because surging demand from the AI industry has triggered one of the most severe supply shortages ever. Therefore, Micron's staggering earnings growth may not be sustainable once more production capacity comes online over the next couple of years. Wall Street is quite good at looking past short-term shocks to a company's financial results (both positive and negative), which is why Micron's P/E ratio is so low right now. While demand for GPUs also exceeds supply, these prices have been far less volatile. That makes Nvidia's financial results more predictable, which is why I think its stock will deliver more upside than Micron's from here.
